title: Computer & Information Sciences
subtitle: Computation, algorithms, data structures & AI
methodology: METHODOLOGY · Every entry anchors to a landmark paper, algorithm, system or theoretical text. The hint explains the idea in plain language; the note names the primary publication and flags impact, subsequent revisions and known controversies. Studies and effects carry a replicability badge — 🟢 confirmed · 🟡 nuanced or contested · 🔴 failed or severely undermined. Map pins mark the institution where the work was carried out.

categories.label: Theories & Models
categories.subtitle: The frameworks that organise computation, complexity, information and system design

entries.label: Church-Turing Thesis
entries.loc: King's College, University of Cambridge, Cambridge, England
entries.hint: Any function that can be computed by a systematic, mechanical procedure can be computed by a Turing Machine — and equivalently by Church's lambda calculus. This is not a theorem but a thesis about the nature of computation itself: it defines what 'algorithm' means by equating all reasonable formalisms for mechanical calculation. Every computer, smartphone, and cloud server is a physical instantiation of this idea.
entries.note: Turing, A. M. (1936). 'On Computable Numbers, with an Application to the Entscheidungsproblem.' Proceedings of the London Mathematical Society, 42, 230–265. Church, A. (1936). 'An Unsolvable Problem of Elementary Number Theory.' American Journal of Mathematics, 58, 345–363. The thesis remains unproven — it is a claim about physical reality, not a mathematical theorem — but is universally accepted: no computational model has been found that exceeds Turing Machine power. Proposals for hypercomputation (oracle machines, analog computation) have not yielded physically realisable counterexamples.

entries.label: Von Neumann Architecture
entries.loc: Institute for Advanced Study, Princeton, New Jersey
entries.hint: The dominant design paradigm for general-purpose computers: a single shared memory stores both program instructions and data; a CPU fetches instructions sequentially (the program counter) and executes them. First described in the First Draft of a Report on the EDVAC (1945), the architecture underlies virtually every computer built since, from microcontrollers to supercomputers.
entries.note: von Neumann, J. (1945). 'First Draft of a Report on the EDVAC.' US Army Ordnance report, reprinted IEEE Annals of the History of Computing (1993), 15(4), 27–75. Authorship is contested: Eckert and Mauchly argued the stored-program concept was theirs and von Neumann took credit by writing the report. The 'von Neumann bottleneck' — CPU-memory bandwidth as the performance ceiling — is addressed by modern caching, SIMD units, and in-memory computing architectures, but the fundamental model has not been displaced.

entries.label: Shannon's Information Theory
entries.loc: Bell Telephone Laboratories, Murray Hill, New Jersey
entries.hint: Shannon defined information as the resolution of uncertainty: the bit is the answer to a single yes/no question. His noisy-channel coding theorem proved that any communication channel has a maximum error-free transmission rate (channel capacity), and that error-correcting codes can approach this limit arbitrarily closely. Every data compression algorithm, wireless standard, and digital storage medium rests on these results.
entries.note: Shannon, C. E. (1948). 'A Mathematical Theory of Communication.' Bell System Technical Journal, 27, 379–423, 623–656. Shannon, C. E. & Weaver, W. (1949). The Mathematical Theory of Communication. University of Illinois Press. The Shannon-Hartley theorem C = B log₂(1 + S/N) governs every wireless data link. Shannon explicitly noted the theory applies to symbol transmission only, not to meaning — a distinction often ignored when information theory is applied to biology, economics, or cognition.

entries.label: Computational Complexity & Cook's Theorem
entries.hint: Cook proved that the Boolean satisfiability problem (SAT) is NP-complete: every problem whose solution can be verified quickly can be reduced to SAT. This establishes a central hierarchy — P (solvable quickly), NP (verifiable quickly), NP-complete (the hardest NP problems). Whether P = NP — whether every easily verified problem is also easily solved — is the most important open question in mathematics and computer science.
entries.note: Cook, S. A. (1971). 'The Complexity of Theorem-Proving Procedures.' Proceedings of the 3rd Annual ACM Symposium on Theory of Computing, 151–158. Independently formulated by Levin, L. A. (1973) in the USSR, giving rise to the Cook-Levin theorem. Karp, R. M. (1972). 'Reducibility Among Combinatorial Problems.' Complexity of Computer Computations, 85–103 — extended the result to 21 classical problems. The Clay Mathematics Institute lists P vs NP as one of seven Millennium Prize Problems, with a USD 1 million prize for resolution.

entries.label: Structured Programming
entries.loc: Eindhoven University of Technology, Eindhoven, Netherlands
entries.hint: Dijkstra argued that unrestricted goto statements make programs impossible to reason about, and that all program logic can be expressed with only three control structures: sequence, conditional, and loop. The Böhm-Jacopini theorem proved this formally. Structured programming made software engineering possible: programs became verifiable, testable, and maintainable rather than inscrutable spaghetti code.
entries.note: Dijkstra, E. W. (1968). 'Go To Statement Considered Harmful.' Communications of the ACM, 11(3), 147–148. Böhm, C. & Jacopini, G. (1966). 'Flow Diagrams, Turing Machines and Languages with Only Two Formation Rules.' Communications of the ACM, 9(5), 366–371 — the formal proof. The letter triggered a sustained debate (Rubin 1987 wrote 'GOTO Considered Harmful Considered Harmful'); modern languages have settled on structured control flow while retaining break, continue, and exception-handling as disciplined exits.

entries.label: Relational Model of Data
entries.loc: IBM San Jose Research Laboratory, San Jose, California
entries.hint: Codd proposed storing data in relations (tables) and querying it via a declarative language grounded in mathematical set theory. The user specifies what data is needed; the database engine decides how to retrieve it. This separation of logical data structure from physical storage allowed databases to be reorganised, replicated, and optimised without rewriting application code — the foundational abstraction behind every SQL database.
entries.note: Codd, E. F. (1970). 'A Relational Model of Data for Large Shared Data Banks.' Communications of the ACM, 13(6), 377–387. Turing Award 1981 (Codd). The relational model was initially resisted within IBM (it threatened the existing IMS product) and first commercialised by Oracle (1979). Critique: the object-relational impedance mismatch — tension between relational tables and object-oriented code — motivated NoSQL databases (MongoDB, Cassandra); however, relational databases remain dominant for transactional workloads.

entries.label: CAP Theorem
entries.hint: Brewer conjectured — and Gilbert & Lynch later proved — that a distributed data system can simultaneously guarantee at most two of three properties: Consistency (every read reflects the most recent write), Availability (every request receives a response), and Partition tolerance (the system continues operating despite network splits). Real distributed databases must choose which two to prioritise — Cassandra (AP) vs HBase (CP) — a choice that drives distributed systems architecture.
entries.note: Brewer, E. A. (2000). 'Towards Robust Distributed Systems.' Proceedings of the 19th Annual ACM Symposium on Principles of Distributed Computing. Gilbert, S. & Lynch, N. (2002). 'Brewer's Conjecture and the Feasibility of Consistent, Available, Partition-Tolerant Web Services.' ACM SIGACT News, 33(2), 51–59. Brewer (2012) himself noted the theorem is often over-applied: partition tolerance is non-optional (partitions always occur eventually), so the real trade-off is between consistency and availability during a partition. The PACELC model (Abadi 2012) extends CAP to include latency vs consistency under normal operation.

entries.label: Physical Symbol System Hypothesis
entries.hint: Newell and Simon proposed that a physical symbol system — any device that manipulates symbols according to rules — has the necessary and sufficient conditions for general intelligent action. This underpinned Good Old-Fashioned AI (GOFAI): expert systems, logic programming, and automated theorem proving. It remains the foundational claim contested in AI philosophy: does intelligence require symbol manipulation, or is something more (embodiment, statistical pattern-matching) necessary?
entries.note: Newell, A. & Simon, H. A. (1976). 'Computer Science as Empirical Inquiry: Symbols and Search.' Communications of the ACM, 19(3), 113–126 — their 1975 Turing Award lecture. Critique: Dreyfus, H. L. (1972). What Computers Can't Do. Harper & Row — argued that human intelligence is embodied and contextual. Searle, J. R. (1980). 'Minds, Brains, and Programs.' Behavioral and Brain Sciences, 3, 417–424 — the Chinese Room argument. The connectionist and deep learning revolutions provided an empirical alternative that achieves intelligent behaviour without explicit symbol manipulation.

entries.label: PAC Learning Theory
entries.hint: Valiant asked: under what conditions can a learning algorithm generalise from training examples to new cases? He defined a Probably Approximately Correct (PAC) learner as one that, given enough examples, outputs a hypothesis correct on all but an ε fraction of instances with probability at least 1-δ. PAC learning theory gave machine learning its first rigorous mathematical foundation, bounding how many training examples are needed for a given hypothesis class.
entries.note: Valiant, L. G. (1984). 'A Theory of the Learnable.' Communications of the ACM, 27(11), 1134–1142. Turing Award 2010 (Valiant). The VC dimension (Vapnik & Chervonenkis 1971) provides a complementary complexity measure for hypothesis classes. Limitation: modern deep learning operates in overparameterised regimes where classical PAC bounds are vacuous — they predict extreme overfitting that does not occur in practice. Understanding why deep networks generalise despite vastly exceeding classical sample-complexity bounds is an open problem (Bartlett et al. 2020, Journal of the ACM).

entries.label: Public-Key Cryptography
entries.hint: Diffie and Hellman showed that two parties can establish a shared secret over an insecure channel with no prior contact — using a mathematical trapdoor function that is easy to compute forward but practically impossible to reverse. This solved the key distribution problem that had stymied cryptography for millennia, and enabled the entire architecture of internet security: HTTPS, TLS, SSH, and digital signatures all depend on this principle.
entries.note: Diffie, W. & Hellman, M. E. (1976). 'New Directions in Cryptography.' IEEE Transactions on Information Theory, 22(6), 644–654. Turing Award 2015 (Diffie and Hellman). GCHQ independently discovered equivalent concepts (Ellis 1969, Cocks 1973) but the work remained classified until 1997. RSA (Rivest, Shamir, Adleman 1977) provided the first practical realisation. Post-quantum urgency: Shor's algorithm (1994) can factor RSA keys in polynomial time on a sufficiently large quantum computer, making post-quantum cryptography standardisation (NIST 2022–2024) critical.

entries.label: Packet Switching Theory
entries.hint: Baran proposed dividing messages into fixed-size blocks independently routed through a distributed network and reassembled at the destination — a radical departure from circuit-switching, where a dedicated line is held open for the entire call. If any node is destroyed, blocks reroute automatically via other paths. Davies independently reached the same idea in the UK and coined the term "packet." ARPANET adopted packet switching in 1969, making this the conceptual foundation of the internet.
entries.note: Baran, P. (1964). "On Distributed Communications." RAND Corporation Technical Reports RM-3103-PR through RM-3420-PR (11 volumes). Davies, D. W. (1966). "Proposal for a Digital Communication Network." NPL internal report — coined the term "packet." Baran and Davies worked independently and only met after publishing. AT&T engineers considered packet switching technically inferior and commercially uninteresting. Leonard Kleinrock (UCLA, 1961) published queuing-theory foundations before either, though his early models used a different topology. ARPANET's implementation by BBN (1969) was the first large-scale realisation and is cited directly in Davies's later survey work.

categories.label: Landmark Studies
categories.subtitle: The defining experiments — and exactly what they found

entries.label: Turing's Imitation Game (Computing Machinery and Intelligence)
entries.hint: Turing proposed a test for machine intelligence: if an interrogator, communicating by text only, cannot reliably distinguish a machine from a human, the machine can be said to think. The paper also anticipated and answered nine objections to machine intelligence, including theological, mathematical, and consciousness-based arguments. The Turing Test defined the research agenda for AI for decades and remains the most cited framing of the question of machine cognition.
entries.note: Turing, A. M. (1950). 'Computing Machinery and Intelligence.' Mind, 59(236), 433–460. The test has been 'passed' by ELIZA (Weizenbaum 1966) and various chatbots under informal conditions, but critics argue it tests human gullibility rather than machine intelligence (Searle 1980; Harnad 1991). The Loebner Prize (1990–2020) ran annual Turing Test competitions. Turing explicitly stated the test was a 'convenient criterion' rather than a definition of thought — a distinction often lost in popular accounts. Repl badge: mixed — the test is widely used but increasingly regarded as insufficient.

entries.label: ENIAC: First General-Purpose Electronic Computer
entries.hint: ENIAC (Electronic Numerical Integrator and Computer) was the first large-scale, general-purpose electronic digital computer, operational in 1945 at Penn. It could be reprogrammed for different problems by physically rerouting cables and setting 3,000 switches. It computed ballistic trajectory tables in seconds that took human computers days. ENIAC demonstrated that electronic computation was faster than mechanical alternatives by orders of magnitude.
entries.note: Eckert, J. P. & Mauchly, J. W. (1945). 'Automatic High-Speed Computing: A Progress Report on the ENIAC.' Moore School of Electrical Engineering, University of Pennsylvania. ENIAC contained 17,468 vacuum tubes, weighed 27 tons, and consumed 150 kW. A modern smartphone performs roughly 10¹² times as many operations per second. The question of who deserves priority for the electronic computer remains disputed: Atanasoff-Berry Computer (1942) preceded ENIAC but was non-programmable; Colossus (1943, Bletchley Park) was programmable but single-purpose and classified.

entries.label: ARPANET First Host-to-Host Message
entries.loc: University of California Los Angeles, Los Angeles, California
entries.hint: On 29 October 1969, Charley Kline at UCLA attempted to transmit 'LOGIN' to the Stanford Research Institute — the system crashed after 'LO', making the first internet message 'lo'. The ARPANET demonstrated that packet-switching could reliably transmit data across heterogeneous networks — a radical departure from the circuit-switching model of telephone networks. It directly evolved into the modern internet.
entries.note: Roberts, L. G. (1970). 'Computer Network Development to Achieve Resource Sharing.' Proceedings of the AFIPS Spring Joint Computer Conference, 36, 543–549. The ARPANET was funded by ARPA (now DARPA) and grew from 4 nodes in 1969 to 111 by 1977. Email was added by Ray Tomlinson in 1971 (including the @ convention). The transition from ARPANET to the modern internet involved the adoption of TCP/IP on 1 January 1983 ('Flag Day'), retirement of the NCP protocol, and the opening of the network to commercial providers in 1993.

entries.label: Unix Operating System & C Language
entries.loc: Bell Telephone Laboratories, Murray Hill, New Jersey
entries.hint: Ken Thompson, Dennis Ritchie, and colleagues at Bell Labs built Unix as a small, general-purpose operating system for a discarded PDP-7. Thompson wrote it in three weeks. Ritchie developed the C language to rewrite it portably. Unix introduced the pipe (|) for chaining programs, the filesystem hierarchy, and the philosophy that programs should do one thing well. It became the conceptual foundation of Linux, macOS, Android, and iOS.
entries.note: Ritchie, D. M. & Thompson, K. (1974). 'The UNIX Time-Sharing System.' Communications of the ACM, 17(7), 365–375. Ritchie, D. M. (1978). 'The C Programming Language.' Bell System Technical Journal — later formalised in Kernighan, B. W. & Ritchie, D. M. (1978). The C Programming Language. Prentice Hall. ACM Turing Award 1983 (Thompson and Ritchie jointly). Bell Labs distributed Unix source code to universities for free until AT&T commercialised it (1979), fuelling the BSD distributions and the eventual open-source movement.

entries.label: Quicksort (Hoare)
entries.hint: Hoare's Quicksort partitions an array around a pivot element, recursively sorts the two halves, and produces a fully sorted array. On average it requires O(n log n) comparisons — faster in practice than mergesort despite an O(n²) worst case — and sorts in-place, making it memory-efficient. Quicksort or its derivatives are the default sorting algorithm in the standard libraries of C, C++, Java, Python, and most other languages.
entries.note: Hoare, C. A. R. (1962). 'Quicksort.' Computer Journal, 5(1), 10–16. Knuth, D. E. (1998). The Art of Computer Programming, Vol. 3: Sorting and Searching, 2nd ed., Addison-Wesley — provides the definitive average-case analysis. Worst case O(n²) occurs with sorted or reverse-sorted input on naive pivot choice; randomised pivot selection or median-of-three makes pathological cases exponentially rare. Introsort (Musser 1997) — the variant used in C++ STL — switches to heapsort when recursion depth exceeds a threshold, guaranteeing O(n log n) worst case.

entries.label: RSA Public-Key Encryption Algorithm
entries.hint: Rivest, Shamir, and Adleman provided the first practical realisation of public-key cryptography: any message encrypted with a recipient's public key can only be decrypted by their private key, which is derived from the product of two large primes. Breaking RSA requires factoring the product of those primes — a problem believed (but not proven) to require exponential time on classical computers. RSA underpins HTTPS certificates, digital signatures, and email encryption.
entries.note: Rivest, R. L., Shamir, A. & Adleman, L. (1978). 'A Method for Obtaining Digital Signatures and Public-Key Cryptosystems.' Communications of the ACM, 21(2), 120–126. Turing Award 2002 (Rivest, Shamir, Adleman). An equivalent algorithm was independently discovered by Clifford Cocks at GCHQ in 1973 but remained classified until 1997. Security depends on the hardness of integer factorisation: keys of 2048 bits are considered secure against classical computers as of 2024. Shor's quantum algorithm (1994) would render RSA insecure on a sufficiently large quantum computer, motivating post-quantum alternatives (lattice-based cryptography).

entries.label: World Wide Web (Berners-Lee)
entries.hint: Tim Berners-Lee, a physicist at CERN, combined three existing ideas — hypertext, the internet, and a domain name system — to propose a distributed information system in which documents link to each other via URLs over HTTP. The first website went live on 20 December 1990. CERN made the web technology royalty-free in 1993, enabling explosive growth: from one website in 1991 to over 1 billion by 2014.
entries.note: Berners-Lee, T. & Cailliau, R. (1990). 'WorldWideWeb: Proposal for a HyperText Project.' CERN internal proposal. Berners-Lee, T. (1992). 'The World-Wide Web.' Computer Networks and ISDN Systems, 25(4-5), 454–459. Berners-Lee received the Turing Award in 2016. The web is distinct from the internet (the underlying network): it is an application layer built on TCP/IP. Berners-Lee has advocated for a 'Solid' decentralised web to address data ownership problems created by platform monopolisation.

entries.label: Deep Blue Defeats World Chess Champion (Kasparov)
entries.loc: IBM Research, Yorktown Heights, New York
entries.hint: IBM's Deep Blue defeated Garry Kasparov — the reigning world chess champion — in a six-game match in May 1997, the first time a computer had beaten a human world champion under standard tournament conditions. Deep Blue searched up to 200 million positions per second using alpha-beta pruning and hand-crafted chess evaluation functions. It demonstrated that narrow AI could surpass human expertise in a domain long considered a benchmark of human intelligence.
entries.note: Campbell, M., Hoane, A. J. & Hsu, F.-H. (2002). 'Deep Blue.' Artificial Intelligence, 134(1-2), 57–83. Deep Blue used custom VLSI chess chips, evaluated 200 million positions per second, and employed a handcrafted evaluation function developed with grandmaster advice. Kasparov accused IBM of cheating after Game 2 (an unusual move he couldn't understand; IBM later said it was a bug that caused a random move). IBM declined a rematch and dismantled the machine. The match is widely cited as the end of chess as a benchmark for AI — it demonstrated strength without general intelligence.

entries.label: PageRank Algorithm (Brin & Page)
entries.hint: Brin and Page proposed ranking web pages by the number and quality of pages linking to them — treating links as votes, with votes from highly-ranked pages weighted more heavily. The PageRank score is the stationary distribution of a random walk over the web graph. This link-based ranking dramatically outperformed keyword-matching search engines and became the foundation of Google, transforming how knowledge is navigated on the web.
entries.note: Brin, S. & Page, L. (1998). 'The Anatomy of a Large-Scale Hypertextual Web Search Engine.' Proceedings of the 7th International Conference on World Wide Web, 107–117. Google was incorporated in September 1998. PageRank was named both after web pages and after Larry Page. Modern Google uses hundreds of ranking signals beyond PageRank; the algorithm has evolved significantly since 1998 and PageRank values are no longer publicly reported. Search-engine optimisation (SEO) emerged as an industry specifically to game ranking algorithms.

entries.label: AlphaGo: Deep Reinforcement Learning Defeats Go Champion
entries.hint: AlphaGo combined deep convolutional neural networks (trained on human games and by self-play) with Monte Carlo tree search to defeat Lee Sedol, a 9-dan Go professional, 4-1 in March 2016. Go has more board positions than atoms in the observable universe; brute-force search is impossible. AlphaGo's success — and AlphaZero's subsequent mastery of chess and Go without human game data — demonstrated that deep reinforcement learning could find superhuman strategies in complex domains from scratch.
entries.note: Silver, D. et al. (2016). 'Mastering the Game of Go with Deep Neural Networks and Tree Search.' Nature, 529, 484–489. AlphaGo Zero (Silver et al. 2017, Nature, 550, 354–359) learned purely from self-play with no human game data and surpassed the original AlphaGo. AlphaZero (Silver et al. 2018, Science, 362, 1140–1144) mastered chess, shogi, and Go with the same algorithm. The Go community noted that AlphaGo discovered novel opening strategies (moves 37 and 78 in Game 2) that professional players subsequently adopted.

entries.label: GPT-3: Large Language Model at Scale
entries.hint: GPT-3 is a 175-billion parameter transformer language model trained on approximately 570 GB of text from the internet. With no task-specific training, it could write essays, answer questions, generate code, and perform translation by conditioning on a few examples in the prompt (few-shot learning). GPT-3 demonstrated that scaling a single architecture across more data and compute could produce qualitative capability jumps — a scaling law that has since driven the development of all large language models.
entries.note: Brown, T. et al. (2020). 'Language Models are Few-Shot Learners.' Advances in Neural Information Processing Systems, 33, 1877–1901. Scaling laws: Kaplan, J. et al. (2020). 'Scaling Laws for Neural Language Models.' arXiv:2001.08361. GPT-3 was accessed via API only; GPT-4 (2023) and subsequent models have made capabilities more accessible. Limitations documented: factual hallucination, inconsistency, sensitivity to prompt phrasing, and demographic bias from training data. Repl: mixed — few-shot capabilities are real and robust, but reports of emergent reasoning are contested (Schaeffer et al. 2023).

entries.label: The Mother of All Demos (Engelbart)
entries.loc: Stanford Research Institute, Menlo Park, California
entries.hint: In a single 90-minute presentation at the Fall Joint Computer Conference in 1968, Douglas Engelbart live-demonstrated a computer with a mouse, editable documents, hyperlinks, real-time collaborative editing between remote users, and video conferencing — none of which any audience member had seen before. The demo is the bridge between ENIAC-era batch computing and the personal, interactive, networked computing we use today. Every graphic interface, every wiki, every video call traces a direct lineage to this room.
entries.note: Engelbart, D. C. (1968). 'A Research Center for Augmenting Human Intellect.' Proceedings of the AFIPS Fall Joint Computer Conference, 33, 395-410. The full 100-minute footage is archived at the Doug Engelbart Institute (engelbart.org). Engelbart's NLS (oN-Line System) at SRI's Augmentation Research Center originated every concept demonstrated; commercial products followed 10-20 years later — the mouse with the Xerox Star (1981), hypertext with the WWW (1991). Engelbart received the Turing Award in 1997. The demo is widely considered the most consequential single presentation in the history of computing.

entries.label: Perceptron & the First AI Winter (Minsky-Papert)
entries.hint: Frank Rosenblatt's Perceptron (1957) was the first learning neural model, triggering sweeping predictions that machine intelligence was years away. Minsky and Papert's 1969 mathematical proof showed that a single-layer perceptron cannot compute the XOR function — a fatal limitation ruling out most interesting tasks — and that Rosenblatt had no method for training multi-layer networks that would overcome it. Research funding collapsed, beginning the first AI Winter. The problem lay dormant until backpropagation (1986) demonstrated that multi-layer networks could learn XOR and far harder functions.
entries.note: Rosenblatt, F. (1958). 'The Perceptron: A Probabilistic Model for Information Storage and Organization in the Brain.' Psychological Review, 65(6), 386-408. Minsky, M. L. & Papert, S. A. (1969). Perceptrons: An Introduction to Computational Geometry. MIT Press. The XOR limitation is mathematically definitive: no linear classifier can separate XOR-distributed data. Rumelhart, D. E., Hinton, G. E. & Williams, R. J. (1986). 'Learning Representations by Back-propagating Errors.' Nature, 323, 533-536 — solved the multi-layer training problem. A second AI winter followed in the late 1980s after expert systems underdelivered; the current deep learning era is the third wave, sparked by AlexNet (2012).

entries.label: Nakamoto Consensus & the Blockchain (Bitcoin)
entries.hint: Satoshi Nakamoto's 2008 whitepaper proposed the first solution to the Byzantine Generals Problem in a permissionless, adversarial network: proof-of-work allows anonymous participants to agree on a canonical transaction history with no trusted authority. The blockchain — a cryptographically chained sequence of transaction blocks — makes rewriting history computationally prohibitive. Whether understood as distributed systems breakthrough, monetary experiment, or environmental liability, the paper is the most cited CS preprint of the 21st century.
entries.note: Nakamoto, S. (2008). 'Bitcoin: A Peer-to-Peer Electronic Cash System.' bitcoin.org/bitcoin.pdf. The identity of Satoshi Nakamoto remains unknown. The theoretical foundation is: Lamport, L., Shostak, R. & Pease, M. (1982). 'The Byzantine Generals Problem.' ACM Transactions on Programming Languages and Systems, 4(3), 382-401. Paxos (Lamport 1989) solved Byzantine consensus in trusted networks; Nakamoto solved it without trust. Repl: mixed — the consensus mechanism works as described, but Bitcoin's energy use (~100 TWh/year, comparable to a mid-sized country), 7 transactions/second throughput ceiling, and use in ransomware are well-documented. Ethereum (2015) extended the model with programmable smart contracts; its shift to proof-of-stake (2022) reduced energy use by ~99.95%.

entries.label: "Attention Is All You Need" — Transformer Architecture
entries.hint: Vaswani et al. replaced recurrent neural networks with a purely attention-based architecture: self-attention lets every position in a sequence attend to every other position in parallel, eliminating the sequential bottleneck of RNNs and enabling training on far larger datasets. The transformer became the universal backbone of NLP (BERT, GPT, T5), computer vision (ViT), and protein folding (AlphaFold 2) — the most consequential single architecture shift in deep learning history, generating over 100,000 citations within eight years.
entries.note: Vaswani, A. et al. (2017). "Attention Is All You Need." Advances in Neural Information Processing Systems, 30 (NeurIPS). BERT (Devlin et al. 2018) applied transformers to language understanding; GPT-2/3/4 to generation; ViT (Dosovitskiy et al. 2020) to images; AlphaFold 2 (Jumper et al. 2021, Nature) to protein structure. Limitations: standard attention has O(n²) complexity in sequence length, constraining context windows; addressed by sparse attention (Longformer, BigBird) and linear approximations. The architecture introduced no new mathematical primitive — the key insight was that recurrence was unnecessary and that attention alone sufficed. Repl: robust — the performance advantage over RNNs is definitively established across virtually every sequence modelling benchmark.

entries.label: Haber & Stornetta: Cryptographic Chain of Blocks
entries.hint: Haber and Stornetta published the first protocol for cryptographically tamper-evident timestamping: each document's timestamp incorporates the hash of the previous one, forming a chain where any retroactive alteration is immediately detectable. A later refinement added Merkle trees to batch thousands of documents into a single hash anchor. Satoshi Nakamoto cited three Haber-Stornetta papers in the Bitcoin whitepaper — this chain-of-hashes structure is the direct intellectual precursor to the blockchain.
entries.note: Haber, S. & Stornetta, W. S. (1991). "How to Time-Stamp a Digital Document." Journal of Cryptology, 3(2), 99–111. Bayer, D., Haber, S. & Stornetta, W. S. (1993). "Improving the Efficiency and Reliability of Digital Time-Stamping." In Sequences II: Methods in Communication, Security, and Computer Science, 329–334 — introduced Merkle trees. Satoshi Nakamoto's Bitcoin whitepaper (2008) cites three Haber-Stornetta papers in its eight-entry reference list, making this the most directly acknowledged intellectual lineage in cryptocurrency. The commercial product Surety began publishing weekly SHA-256 hash anchors in The New York Times classifieds in 1995 — arguably the first operational blockchain, predating Bitcoin by 13 years. Repl: robust — the cryptographic integrity properties are mathematically established.

entries.label: MYCIN Expert System
entries.hint: MYCIN was the first expert system to demonstrably match specialist clinical performance: it diagnosed bacterial blood infections and recommended antibiotics via roughly 600 IF-THEN production rules encoded from interviews with infectious-disease physicians. In controlled evaluation MYCIN outperformed medical residents and equalled attending physicians. It demonstrated that structured domain knowledge could be codified and applied computationally — and simultaneously raised questions about transparency, liability, and the brittleness of rule-based systems that remain live today.
entries.note: Shortliffe, E. H. (1976). Computer-Based Medical Consultations: MYCIN. American Elsevier. Controlled evaluation: Yu, V. L. et al. (1979). "Antimicrobial selection by a computer." JAMA, 242(12), 1279–1282 — MYCIN outperformed four infectious-disease faculty, five residents, and ten medical students on bacteraemia cases. MYCIN was never deployed clinically; legal liability for machine-assisted medical decisions was uncharted territory. Its knowledge-acquisition methodology became the template for the 1980s expert-system industry (XCON, CADUCEUS, PROSPECTOR). Limitation: each new domain required a complete re-engineering effort; systems could not learn from cases or generalise across domains. The failure of this scaling model contributed to the second AI winter. Repl: mixed — clinical effectiveness demonstrated in one controlled setting; generalisability and deployment-readiness were never achieved.

categories.label: Effects & Phenomena
categories.subtitle: Named, replicable patterns in computational and information systems

entries.label: Moore's Law
entries.hint: Gordon Moore observed in 1965 that the number of transistors per integrated circuit had doubled roughly every year since 1959, and predicted this trend would continue. Revised to a doubling roughly every two years, 'Moore's Law' drove five decades of exponential improvements in computing speed, memory density, and cost. It has become a self-fulfilling prophecy: the semiconductor industry sets roadmaps to the law and organises R&D accordingly.
entries.note: Moore, G. E. (1965). 'Cramming More Components onto Integrated Circuits.' Electronics, 38(8), 114–117. Moore revised his estimate to a doubling every 24 months in 1975. Moore's Law is an observation about industry practice, not a physical law — it is maintained by coordinated investment rather than fundamental physics. Physical limits (quantum tunnelling, heat dissipation at atomic scales) have slowed transistor density gains since ~2010; improvements now come primarily from architectural innovations (3D stacking, specialised accelerators like GPUs and TPUs). Repl: mixed — the exponential trend has continued but is slowing.

entries.label: Halting Problem Undecidability
entries.loc: King's College, University of Cambridge, Cambridge, England
entries.hint: Turing proved that no general algorithm can determine, for an arbitrary program and input, whether the program will eventually halt or run forever. The proof is by diagonalisation: assuming such a decider exists leads to a contradiction. This established the first hard limit on what computation can achieve — some problems are not just hard but provably unsolvable — and underpins all subsequent results in computability theory.
entries.note: Turing, A. M. (1936). 'On Computable Numbers, with an Application to the Entscheidungsproblem.' Proceedings of the London Mathematical Society, 42, 230–265. The diagonalisation proof is one of the most elegant arguments in mathematics. Rice's Theorem (1953) generalises the result: no non-trivial semantic property of programs is decidable. Practical implications: static analysis tools for infinite loops, virus detection, and formal verification are necessarily incomplete — they either miss some cases or produce false positives.

entries.label: Amdahl's Law
entries.hint: Amdahl showed that the maximum speedup from parallelising a program is limited by its sequential fraction: if 5% of a task must run sequentially, no amount of parallel hardware can ever achieve more than 20× speedup, regardless of the number of processors. The law explains why multicore CPUs deliver diminishing returns for programs that are not designed for parallelism, and why code optimisation should target the bottleneck rather than the common case.
entries.note: Amdahl, G. M. (1967). 'Validity of the Single Processor Approach to Achieving Large-Scale Computing Capabilities.' Proceedings of the AFIPS Spring Joint Computer Conference, 30, 483–485. Gustafson's Law (1988) offered a more optimistic counterpoint: for workloads that scale with the number of processors, efficiency can remain high as cores are added. Together, the two laws frame the fundamental trade-off in parallel systems design. Modern chip architects use Amdahl's Law to decide which components to accelerate with specialised hardware (GPU, NPU, DSP).

entries.label: Metcalfe's Law
entries.hint: Robert Metcalfe, co-inventor of Ethernet, observed that the value of a telecommunications network is proportional to the square of the number of connected users. With n nodes, there are n(n-1)/2 possible pairwise connections, so value grows as n². This explains why network effects make dominant platforms increasingly hard to displace and why internet adoption accelerated non-linearly once a critical mass of users was connected.
entries.note: Metcalfe, B. (1995). 'Metcalfe's Law: A Network Becomes More Valuable as It Reaches More Users.' Infoworld, 17(40), 53. The law was retroactively attributed to Metcalfe's unpublished Ethernet slide from around 1980. Briscoe, B., Odlyzko, A. & Tilly, B. (2006). 'Metcalfe's Law is Wrong.' IEEE Spectrum, 43(7), 34–39 — argued network value grows as n log(n), not n², because most connections are irrelevant to a given user. The qualitative insight (network value grows superlinearly with users) is well established; the exact exponent is contested. Repl: mixed.

entries.label: Fitts's Law
entries.hint: Fitts showed that the time to move a pointing device to a target is a logarithmic function of the ratio of target distance to target width: T = a + b log₂(2D/W). A distant, small target takes longer to hit than a nearby, large one — a quantitative law governing human motor control. Fitts's Law is the bedrock of HCI design: it explains why desktop taskbars belong at screen edges (infinite width) and why small touch targets are slow to tap.
entries.note: Fitts, P. M. (1954). 'The Information Capacity of the Human Motor System in Controlling the Amplitude of Movement.' Journal of Experimental Psychology, 47(6), 381–391. MacKenzie, I. S. (1992). 'Fitts' Law as a Research and Design Tool in Human-Computer Interaction.' Human-Computer Interaction, 7(1), 91–139 — adapted the law for 2D pointing. The law has been validated across pointing devices (mouse, touchscreen, eye-gaze, brain-computer interface) with high reproducibility (R² typically > 0.98). Apple's original Macintosh menu bar at the screen edge is a direct application.

entries.label: No Free Lunch Theorem
entries.loc: NASA Ames Research Center, Moffett Field, California
entries.hint: Wolpert and Macready proved that averaged over all possible problems, any two optimisation algorithms perform identically — no algorithm is universally superior. To outperform random search on any specific class of problems, an algorithm must incorporate assumptions (inductive biases) about that class. The theorem explains why there is no single best machine learning algorithm: a method that works well on image recognition may perform poorly on time-series forecasting.
entries.note: Wolpert, D. H. & Macready, W. G. (1997). 'No Free Lunch Theorems for Optimization.' IEEE Transactions on Evolutionary Computation, 1(1), 67–82. The result applies to the uniform distribution over all possible fitness functions — a distribution that does not reflect any real-world problem. Critics (e.g., Igel & Toussaint 2005) note that if the target distribution of problems is not uniform (and real-world problems are not), free lunches may exist for that distribution. The theorem is most useful as a reminder that empirical benchmarking is necessary and that assumptions must be made explicit.

entries.label: Turing Completeness
entries.loc: King's College, University of Cambridge, Cambridge, England
entries.hint: A computational system is Turing-complete if it can simulate any Turing Machine — meaning it can compute anything that is computable at all. All general-purpose programming languages (C, Python, JavaScript, SQL with recursive CTEs) are Turing-complete. Surprisingly, so are many informal systems: Conway's Game of Life, Magic: The Gathering, and Minecraft redstone circuits. Turing completeness is both the foundation of software universality and a source of undecidability: any Turing-complete language inherits the halting problem.
entries.note: Turing, A. M. (1936). 'On Computable Numbers.' The concept of Turing completeness was implicit in the original paper and made explicit in subsequent computability theory. A practical consequence: if a domain-specific language becomes Turing-complete (e.g., CSS selectors + HTML, or some query languages), arbitrary programs can be embedded in it, which may introduce unexpected security and performance risks. CSS was shown to be Turing-complete via a reduction to Rule 110 (Ransen 2011).

entries.label: Brooks's Law (Mythical Man-Month)
entries.hint: Fred Brooks observed from managing the IBM OS/360 project that adding manpower to a late software project makes it later. New developers need time to ramp up; communication overhead grows quadratically with team size; and tasks that appear parallelisable often are not. The Mythical Man-Month popularised the insight that software development is fundamentally different from other engineering: adding resources does not linearly add throughput.
entries.note: Brooks, F. P. (1975). The Mythical Man-Month: Essays on Software Engineering. Addison-Wesley. Revised edition (1995) added 'No Silver Bullet: Essence and Accidents of Software Engineering.' The quadratic communication overhead claim (n(n-1)/2 channels for n developers) is well-established in organisational theory but the magnitude of the slowdown is context-dependent: small, cohesive teams suffer less than large, distributed ones. Agile methodologies (small team iterations) and microservices architecture (independent deployment units) are partly responses to the coordination costs Brooks identified. Repl: mixed.

entries.label: Perfect Secrecy (Shannon's Encryption Theorem)
entries.loc: Bell Telephone Laboratories, Murray Hill, New Jersey
entries.hint: Shannon proved that a cipher achieves perfect secrecy — where ciphertext reveals zero information about plaintext even to an adversary with unlimited computing power — if and only if the key is uniformly random, at least as long as the message, and never reused. The one-time pad is the only cipher meeting these conditions. Shannon's theorem defines the gold standard of secrecy and explains why all practical ciphers (which use shorter keys) must settle for computational security.
entries.note: Shannon, C. E. (1949). 'Communication Theory of Secrecy Systems.' Bell System Technical Journal, 28(4), 656–715. The one-time pad (Vernam cipher, 1917) was retroactively shown to be perfectly secure by this theorem. Perfect secrecy is provably impossible to achieve with a key shorter than the message — this places a fundamental lower bound on key length for unconditional security. All modern ciphers (AES, ChaCha20) are computationally secure, meaning no known algorithm can break them in polynomial time, but in principle a sufficiently powerful adversary could. The theorem motivates post-quantum cryptography: breaking computational assumptions is the threat model.

entries.label: Landauer's Principle
entries.loc: IBM Thomas J. Watson Research Center, Yorktown Heights, New York
entries.hint: Landauer proved that erasing one bit of information must dissipate at minimum kT ln2 of energy — roughly 3 × 10⁻²¹ joules at room temperature — as heat. Logical irreversibility (overwriting a register, clearing memory) has an unavoidable thermodynamic cost. The principle links information theory and the second law of thermodynamics and sets an absolute floor on computing energy efficiency — one that current chips exceed by roughly a million-fold, but which becomes relevant as transistors approach atomic scales.
entries.note: Landauer, R. (1961). "Irreversibility and Heat Generation in the Computing Process." IBM Journal of Research and Development, 5(3), 183–191. Experimentally verified: Bérut, A. et al. (2012). "Experimental verification of Landauer's principle linking information and thermodynamics." Nature, 483, 187–189 — colloidal particles used to erase individual bits; heat dissipation measured at the kT ln2 floor. Bennett, C. H. (1973) showed logically reversible computing is theoretically possible, avoiding the Landauer cost, laying foundations for reversible and quantum computing. The gap between current chip energy per operation (~10⁶ kT) and the Landauer limit (~1 kT) implies substantial efficiency headroom; as devices shrink toward atomic scale the gap narrows significantly.

categories.label: Key Concepts
categories.subtitle: The building blocks and vocabulary every student must command

entries.label: Big-O Notation & Asymptotic Complexity
entries.hint: Big-O notation, introduced in mathematics by Bachmann (1894) and formalised for computer science by Knuth (1968), expresses the growth rate of an algorithm's resource consumption (time or space) as a function of input size, ignoring constant factors. O(n²) means the algorithm's cost grows quadratically. This vocabulary allows developers to reason about performance at scale without running code — distinguishing algorithms that will work in production from those that will not.
entries.note: Knuth, D. E. (1968). The Art of Computer Programming, Vol. 1: Fundamental Algorithms. Addison-Wesley — popularised Big-O for computer science. Bachmann, P. (1894). Die Analytische Zahlentheorie. Teubner — the mathematical origin. Related notations: Ω (lower bound), Θ (tight bound). A common misconception: O(n²) does not mean slow — whether an algorithm is fast depends on the constant factor and the practical values of n. O(n³) matrix multiplication can outperform O(n^2.37) algorithms in practice for moderate n because the constant is enormous in the latter.

entries.label: Recursion
entries.hint: A recursive function is one that calls itself with a smaller subproblem until reaching a base case. McCarthy's Lisp (1960) made recursion the primary control flow mechanism in programming, replacing iteration. Recursion maps cleanly onto divide-and-conquer algorithms (mergesort, quicksort, FFT), tree traversals, and parsing — and is the computational analogue of mathematical induction. Without recursion (and its companion, the call stack), most elegant algorithm designs would be impossible to express.
entries.note: McCarthy, J. (1960). 'Recursive Functions of Symbolic Expressions and Their Computation by Machine, Part I.' Communications of the ACM, 3(4), 184–195 — introduced Lisp and recursion as a programming construct. Ackermann, W. (1928). 'Zum Hilbertschen Aufbau der reellen Zahlen.' Mathematische Annalen, 99, 118–133 — the Ackermann function, a classic example of a computable function that is not primitive recursive. Tail-call optimisation transforms tail-recursive calls into loops, avoiding stack overflow — supported natively in Scheme, Haskell, and Kotlin but not in Python or Java.

entries.label: Abstract Data Type (ADT)
entries.hint: An abstract data type defines a data structure by its operations and their specified behaviour, hiding the implementation details. A Stack is defined by push, pop, and peek — whether it is implemented as an array or linked list is irrelevant to the user. ADTs are the conceptual foundation of encapsulation in object-oriented programming and of interface-based design in software engineering — the principle that code should depend on abstractions, not concretions.
entries.note: Liskov, B. & Zilles, S. (1974). 'Programming with Abstract Data Types.' Proceedings of the ACM SIGPLAN Symposium on Very High Level Languages, 50–59. Liskov received the Turing Award in 2008 for contributions to programming language design and formal specification, including the Liskov Substitution Principle (1994), which states that objects of a subtype must be substitutable for objects of their supertype. ADTs were independently developed by Parnas (1972) via information-hiding modules. The concept directly informs Java interfaces, C++ virtual classes, and Haskell type classes.

entries.label: Semaphore & Mutual Exclusion
entries.loc: Eindhoven University of Technology, Eindhoven, Netherlands
entries.hint: Dijkstra introduced the semaphore in 1965 as a synchronisation primitive: an integer variable that can only be modified via two atomic operations (P/wait and V/signal). Semaphores prevent race conditions when multiple processes share a resource. Together with mutual exclusion, they are the foundational building blocks of concurrent programming — underpinning every operating system scheduler, database transaction manager, and multi-threaded application.
entries.note: Dijkstra, E. W. (1965). 'Solution of a Problem in Concurrent Programming Control.' Communications of the ACM, 8(9), 569 — introduced semaphores and the mutual exclusion problem. Dijkstra (1965) also introduced the Dining Philosophers Problem to illustrate deadlock. Higher-level concurrency primitives (mutexes, monitors, condition variables, message-passing channels) are all built on or designed to replace semaphores. Deadlock — a state where two processes each hold a resource the other needs — is a classic consequence of misused mutual exclusion (Coffman et al. 1971).

entries.label: TCP/IP Protocol Stack
entries.hint: Cerf and Kahn's Transmission Control Protocol / Internet Protocol (TCP/IP) defines how data is broken into packets, addressed, routed across heterogeneous networks, and reassembled reliably at the destination. TCP handles ordering, retransmission of lost packets, and flow control; IP handles addressing and routing. The protocol stack's layered architecture allows any network technology (Ethernet, Wi-Fi, 4G, satellite) to carry internet traffic, making the internet technology-agnostic.
entries.note: Cerf, V. G. & Kahn, R. E. (1974). 'A Protocol for Packet Network Intercommunication.' IEEE Transactions on Communications, 22(5), 637–648. Turing Award 2004 (Cerf and Kahn). TCP/IP replaced NCP on ARPANET on 1 January 1983. The protocol stack is typically described as four layers (link, internet, transport, application) or, per the OSI model, seven. UDP provides an unreliable, low-latency alternative to TCP, used for video streaming and DNS. QUIC (Google 2012, IETF standard 2021) reimplements TCP-like reliability over UDP to reduce connection latency.

entries.label: Backpropagation & Gradient Descent
entries.loc: University of California San Diego, La Jolla, California
entries.hint: Backpropagation applies the chain rule of calculus to compute the gradient of a loss function with respect to every weight in a neural network in a single backward pass. Gradient descent then adjusts each weight proportionally to its gradient, iteratively reducing prediction error. Together, they make training deep neural networks tractable. Virtually every deep learning model — CNNs, RNNs, transformers — is trained by some variant of gradient descent with backpropagation.
entries.note: Rumelhart, D. E., Hinton, G. E. & Williams, R. J. (1986). 'Learning Representations by Back-propagating Errors.' Nature, 323, 533–536 — the paper that popularised backprop in neural networks. The algorithm was independently discovered by Werbos (1974, PhD thesis), LeCun (1985), and Parker (1985). Stochastic gradient descent (SGD) — updating weights after each training example rather than the full dataset — is the practical variant; adaptive methods (Adam, RMSProp) adjust the learning rate per parameter. Vanishing gradients in deep networks were a limiting problem until ReLU activations (2010) and residual connections (He et al. 2016).

entries.label: Zero-Knowledge Proof
entries.hint: A zero-knowledge proof is a protocol by which a prover can convince a verifier that a statement is true — e.g., 'I know the password' — without revealing any information beyond the statement's truth. The prover demonstrates knowledge without disclosure. ZKPs are foundational to privacy-preserving authentication, anonymous credentials, and blockchain privacy protocols (Zcash, Ethereum scaling). The concept formalized a precise mathematical notion of what it means to 'prove without revealing'.
entries.note: Goldwasser, S., Micali, S. & Rackoff, C. (1985). 'The Knowledge Complexity of Interactive Proof Systems.' Proceedings of the 17th Annual ACM Symposium on Theory of Computing, 291–304. Turing Award 2012 (Goldwasser and Micali). The 'Ali Baba cave' thought experiment by Quisquater et al. (1990) is the canonical intuitive explanation. Practical variants: zk-SNARKs (succinct non-interactive arguments of knowledge) are now deployed in Zcash and Ethereum rollups; they allow proofs of computational integrity that are fast to verify even if slow to generate.

entries.label: Hash Tables
entries.hint: A hash table maps keys to values by passing the key through a hash function to produce an array index. Lookup, insertion, and deletion all run in O(1) average time — faster than any comparison-based or tree-based structure. Python dictionaries, JavaScript objects, database indexes, network routing tables, and virtually every caching system use hash tables internally. An algorithm that needs O(1) lookup with arbitrary keys is almost always a hash table.
entries.note: Luhn, H. P. (1953). 'A New Method of Recording and Searching Information.' IBM internal memorandum — the earliest documented use. Morris, R. (1968). 'Scatter Storage Techniques.' Communications of the ACM, 11(1), 38-44 — popularised the term 'hash'. Knuth, D. E. (1998). The Art of Computer Programming, Vol. 3, Section 6.4. The Art of Hashing — the definitive analysis. Collision resolution: chaining (each slot holds a linked list) and open addressing (probe a sequence of slots on collision) are the two main strategies. Worst case is O(n) if all keys map to one bucket; load factor management and a good hash function are critical. Cryptographic hash functions (SHA-256, bcrypt) are a separate family designed for non-invertibility, not speed.

categories.label: Methods & Approaches
categories.subtitle: The tools and paradigms that generate the field's evidence

entries.label: Formal Program Verification (Floyd-Hoare Logic)
entries.hint: Floyd and Hoare developed a formal system for proving program correctness: annotate each program point with a logical assertion (a predicate on program state), and prove that if the precondition holds before a statement executes, the postcondition holds after. Hoare triples {P} C {Q} are the notation. This transforms software correctness from a testing question into a mathematical proof — the approach underlying type systems, model checking, and tools like Dafny, Coq, and Isabelle.
entries.note: Floyd, R. W. (1967). 'Assigning Meanings to Programs.' Proceedings of Symposia in Applied Mathematics, 19, 19–32. Hoare, C. A. R. (1969). 'An Axiomatic Basis for Computer Programming.' Communications of the ACM, 12(10), 576–580. Turing Award 1980 (Hoare). Full formal verification is expensive — the seL4 microkernel proof (Klein et al. 2009, SOSP) required ~200,000 lines of Isabelle proof for 8,700 lines of C. Lightweight variants (type systems, static analysis, model checking) are widely deployed. The CompCert verified C compiler (Leroy 2006) is used in safety-critical avionics.

entries.label: Algorithm Analysis (Asymptotic & Empirical)
entries.hint: Knuth's Art of Computer Programming systematised the analysis of algorithms: count the number of operations as a function of input size, express it in Big-O notation, and use probability theory for average-case analysis. Algorithm analysis allows comparison of approaches before implementation and identification of bottlenecks. It is the core methodology of theoretical computer science, underlying the design of every efficient data structure and sorting algorithm.
entries.note: Knuth, D. E. (1968–1973). The Art of Computer Programming, Vols. 1–3. Addison-Wesley. Turing Award 1974 (Knuth). Volumes 4A (2011) and 4B (2022) on combinatorial algorithms appeared decades later. Empirical algorithm analysis — measuring actual running times on benchmark inputs — complements asymptotic analysis because constant factors matter in practice. The field has extended to competitive analysis (online algorithms, Sleator & Tarjan 1985) and amortised analysis (aggregate cost per operation, also Tarjan 1985).

entries.label: Object-Oriented Programming (OOP)
entries.hint: Dahl and Nygaard's Simula 67 introduced objects — data structures bundled with the methods that operate on them — and inheritance as a way to share behaviour between related types. Smalltalk (Kay, 1972) popularised the paradigm as a philosophy: 'everything is an object'. OOP became the dominant programming paradigm through C++, Java, Python, and Swift. Encapsulation, inheritance, and polymorphism provide the three organising principles of object-oriented design.
entries.note: Dahl, O.-J. & Nygaard, K. (1966). 'SIMULA — An ALGOL-based Simulation Language.' Communications of the ACM, 9(9), 671–678. Turing Award 2001 (Dahl and Nygaard, awarded posthumously in Dahl's case). Kay, A. (1972). 'A Personal Computer for Children of All Ages.' Proceedings of the ACM Annual Conference. The object-oriented paradigm has attracted sustained critique: objects encourage shared mutable state, making concurrent programs difficult to reason about (Haskell, Erlang, and Rust favour immutability and message-passing instead). Composition over inheritance is now standard advice in OOP design.

entries.label: Usability Testing & Cognitive Walkthrough
entries.hint: The cognitive walkthrough (Lewis et al. 1990) is a usability evaluation technique in which evaluators step through a task sequence, asking at each step whether a user would know what to do, recognise the correct action, and interpret feedback correctly. It requires no user testing — just interface inspection against a user-task model. Combined with empirical usability testing (Nielsen & Molich 1990), these methods provide systematic evidence about interface quality before deployment.
entries.note: Lewis, C., Polson, P., Wharton, C. & Rieman, J. (1990). 'Testing a Walkthrough Methodology for Theory-Based Design of Walk-Up-and-Use Interfaces.' Proceedings of CHI '90, 235–242. Nielsen, J. & Molich, R. (1990). 'Heuristic Evaluation of User Interfaces.' Proceedings of CHI '90, 249–256 — proposed 10 usability heuristics (visibility of system status, match with the real world, etc.) that remain widely applied. Empirical usability testing was systematised by Rubin, J. (1994). Handbook of Usability Testing. Wiley. Nielsen's observation that 5 participants find ~85% of usability problems (Nielsen 2000) remains influential though its statistical basis is debated.

entries.label: Cross-Validation & Model Selection
entries.hint: Cross-validation is the standard method for estimating how well a machine learning model will generalise to unseen data: partition the dataset into k folds, train on k-1 folds, evaluate on the held-out fold, and average performance across k iterations. It prevents overfitting — the failure mode where a model memorises training data but fails on new inputs. Cross-validation is also the principal tool for comparing algorithms and selecting hyperparameters.
entries.note: Stone, M. (1974). 'Cross-Validatory Choice and Assessment of Statistical Predictions.' Journal of the Royal Statistical Society: Series B, 36(2), 111–133. Geisser, S. (1975). 'The Predictive Sample Reuse Method with Applications.' Journal of the American Statistical Association, 70(350), 320–328. Leave-one-out cross-validation (LOOCV) and k-fold CV (typically k=5 or k=10) are the standard forms. Nested cross-validation is required for unbiased evaluation when hyperparameters are tuned on the same split used for evaluation — a common methodological error (Cawley & Talbot 2010).

entries.label: Penetration Testing
entries.hint: Penetration testing — authorised simulated attacks on a system to find security vulnerabilities before malicious actors do — was formalised in the 1967 US Department of Defense study 'Security Controls for Computer Systems.' Teams called 'tiger teams' attempted to break into government computer systems; their success revealed fundamental weaknesses. Today, penetration testing is a mandatory component of security certification and a professional discipline, with formal methodologies (PTES, OWASP) and ethical and legal frameworks.
entries.note: Ware, W. H. et al. (1970). 'Security Controls for Computer Systems: Report of Defense Science Board Task Force on Computer Security.' RAND Corporation, R-609-1. Anderson, J. P. (1972). 'Computer Security Technology Planning Study.' US Air Force Electronic Systems Division, ESD-TR-73-51 — defined the threat model still used in security architecture. The EC-Council Certified Ethical Hacker (CEH) and OSCP certifications formalised penetration testing as a profession in the 2000s. Legal framework: in most jurisdictions, penetration testing requires explicit written authorisation; unauthorised testing is a criminal offence under laws such as the US Computer Fraud and Abuse Act.

categories.label: Pioneers & Theorists
categories.subtitle: The researchers who shaped the discipline

entries.label: Ada Lovelace
entries.hint: Lovelace translated an Italian account of Babbage's Analytical Engine and added notes three times longer than the original, including what is widely regarded as the first algorithm intended for machine execution — a method for computing Bernoulli numbers. She also articulated the key insight that the Engine could manipulate any symbols according to rules, not just numbers, anticipating the generality of modern computation by a century.
entries.note: Lovelace, A. A. (1843). 'Notes on L. F. Menabrea's Sketch of the Analytical Engine.' Scientific Memoirs, 3, 666–731. Ada Lovelace Day (second Tuesday of October) commemorates her contributions. Historian Allan Bromley (1990) argued the Bernoulli algorithm contained a significant error, questioning whether it could have run; defenders counter that the error is in Menabrea's original, not Lovelace's correction. The Ada programming language (US DoD, 1980) and AWS Lambda (Lambda Calculus) both take their names from her legacy.

entries.label: Alan Turing
entries.loc: King's College, University of Cambridge, Cambridge, England
entries.hint: Turing's 1936 paper defined the mathematical model of computation, proved the halting problem undecidable, and established the limits of what any algorithm can achieve. During WWII he led the cryptanalysis of the German Enigma machine at Bletchley Park, shortening the war by an estimated two years. His 1950 paper defined the test for machine intelligence that still frames the field. Turing is simultaneously the father of theoretical computer science, AI, and cryptanalysis.
entries.note: Turing, A. M. (1936). 'On Computable Numbers.' PLMS, 42, 230–265. Turing, A. M. (1950). 'Computing Machinery and Intelligence.' Mind, 59, 433–460. Turing was prosecuted for homosexuality in 1952, subjected to chemical castration, and died in 1954 of cyanide poisoning — officially ruled suicide, though disputed. He received a royal pardon in 2013. The Turing Award (ACM, annual since 1966) is the highest distinction in computer science. The Bombe machine Turing co-designed broke Enigma at Bletchley Park; subsequent COLOSSUS work (Flowers et al.) broke Lorenz ciphers.

entries.label: Claude Shannon
entries.loc: Bell Telephone Laboratories, Murray Hill, New Jersey
entries.hint: Shannon's 1948 paper founded information theory by quantifying information as the resolution of uncertainty. He defined entropy, proved the channel capacity theorem, and showed that error-correcting codes could approach the Shannon limit. In 1937, his MIT master's thesis proved that Boolean algebra could design electrical circuits — the bridge between logic and electronics that made digital computers possible.
entries.note: Shannon, C. E. (1948). 'A Mathematical Theory of Communication.' Bell System Technical Journal, 27. Shannon, C. E. (1937). 'A Symbolic Analysis of Relay and Switching Circuits.' Transactions of the American Institute of Electrical Engineers, 57, 713–723. Called 'the most important master's thesis of the 20th century' by Howard Gardner. Shannon was known for riding a unicycle through Bell Labs hallways while juggling. He devoted his later career to juggling machines and maze-solving mice, keeping a deliberate distance from the explosion of applications of his theory.

entries.label: John von Neumann
entries.loc: Institute for Advanced Study, Princeton, New Jersey
entries.hint: Von Neumann's stored-program architecture (EDVAC report, 1945) defined the computer design used in virtually every machine since. He also contributed the minimax theorem in game theory, the mathematical foundations of quantum mechanics, and cellular automata. As a consultant to the Manhattan Project and early computing initiatives, he shaped both the hardware design and the institutional direction of early computing, making him arguably the most influential single figure in the history of the field.
entries.note: von Neumann, J. (1945). 'First Draft of a Report on the EDVAC.' US Army Ordnance. von Neumann, J. (1928). 'Zur Theorie der Gesellschaftsspiele.' Mathematische Annalen, 100, 295–320 — minimax theorem. Authorship of the stored-program concept is contested (Eckert and Mauchly filed patents before von Neumann's report; see Stern 1980). Von Neumann was reportedly able to perform mental arithmetic fast enough to beat early computers on small problems, and could quote historical documents verbatim from memory. He died in 1957 at age 53 from cancer, possibly related to nuclear radiation exposure at Los Alamos.

entries.label: Grace Hopper
entries.hint: Hopper created the first compiler — the A-0 system (1952) — which translated mathematical notation into machine code, replacing the error-prone process of hand-coding machine instructions. She championed the idea that programming languages should be readable by humans and helped develop COBOL, the language that processes the majority of the world's financial transactions to this day. She also popularised the term 'bug' after finding a moth in the Harvard Mark II.
entries.note: Hopper, G. M. (1952). 'The Education of a Computer.' Proceedings of the 1952 ACM Meeting, 243–249 — described the A-0 compiler. COBOL: CODASYL (1960). Common Business-Oriented Language — Hopper served on the technical committee and advocated for English-language syntax. Rear Admiral in the US Navy; she coined the expression 'it's easier to ask forgiveness than permission.' National Medal of Technology (1991); posthumous Presidential Medal of Freedom (2016). The moth taped to the Harvard Mark II logbook (1947) is on display at the Smithsonian; Hopper didn't coin 'bug' (the term predates her) but she popularised the moth story.

entries.label: Edsger Dijkstra
entries.loc: Eindhoven University of Technology, Eindhoven, Netherlands
entries.hint: Dijkstra contributed foundational results across theoretical and practical computer science: the shortest-path algorithm (1959), the semaphore synchronisation primitive (1965), the argument against goto statements (1968), and the structured programming discipline. He was also a forceful critic of computing culture, opposing the word 'computer science' (he preferred 'programming') and arguing that testing can only show the presence of bugs, never their absence — a principle that motivates formal verification.
entries.note: Dijkstra, E. W. (1959). 'A Note on Two Problems in Connexion with Graphs.' Numerische Mathematik, 1(1), 269–271 — the shortest-path algorithm. Dijkstra, E. W. (1965). 'Solution of a Problem in Concurrent Programming Control.' CACM, 8(9), 569. Dijkstra, E. W. (1968). 'Go To Statement Considered Harmful.' CACM, 11(3), 147–148. Turing Award 1972. Dijkstra wrote his papers and manuscripts by hand in a personal shorthand; the EWD archive (University of Texas) contains over 1,300 handwritten manuscripts. He notoriously preferred disciplined mathematical notation to informal argument and considered most software engineering practice sloppy.

entries.label: Ken Thompson & Dennis Ritchie
entries.loc: Bell Telephone Laboratories, Murray Hill, New Jersey
entries.hint: Thompson and Ritchie built Unix and the C programming language at Bell Labs, providing the software infrastructure that underlies modern computing. Thompson designed the Unix architecture, the shell, and the pipe operator. Ritchie designed C to give programmers a portable, systems-level language that could replace assembly. Their collaboration produced not just two artefacts but a design philosophy — small tools, text as a universal interface, everything is a file — that shapes operating systems to this day.
entries.note: Ritchie, D. M. & Thompson, K. (1974). 'The UNIX Time-Sharing System.' CACM, 17(7), 365–375. Kernighan, B. W. & Ritchie, D. M. (1978). The C Programming Language. Prentice Hall. Turing Award 1983 (Thompson and Ritchie jointly). Thompson later co-invented the Go programming language at Google (2009). Ritchie died in October 2011, one week after Steve Jobs — his death was noted far less in mainstream media, prompting reflection on the visibility of foundational infrastructure contributors versus product creators.

entries.label: Edgar F. Codd
entries.loc: IBM San Jose Research Laboratory, San Jose, California
entries.hint: Codd's 1970 paper proposing the relational model transformed data management from a collection of ad hoc file-system tricks into a mathematically principled discipline. He showed that if data is stored in tables with well-defined relations, query languages grounded in first-order logic could retrieve any subset of data declaratively. Every SQL database — Oracle, PostgreSQL, MySQL, SQLite — implements his model, making Codd's work foundational to virtually every organisation that stores data.
entries.note: Codd, E. F. (1970). 'A Relational Model of Data for Large Shared Data Banks.' CACM, 13(6), 377–387. Turing Award 1981. Codd also proposed twelve rules (Codd's rules, 1985) that a database must satisfy to be considered truly relational — most commercial databases do not fully satisfy all twelve. IBM delayed commercialising Codd's work for a decade to protect its existing IMS product; Oracle (Larry Ellison) exploited this delay to become the dominant database vendor. Codd suffered in his later years from disputes over database product claims and was not fully credited during his lifetime.

entries.label: Margaret Hamilton
entries.hint: Hamilton led the team that wrote the flight software for NASA's Apollo program, including the onboard software that landed humans on the Moon. During the Apollo 11 descent, when the navigation computer was overloaded with spurious radar data, Hamilton's priority-interrupt design allowed it to shed lower-priority tasks and complete the critical guidance task — saving the landing. Hamilton coined the term 'software engineering' to argue that software deserved the same rigour as other engineering disciplines.
entries.note: Hamilton, M. H. & Zeldin, S. (1976). 'Higher Order Software — A Methodology for Defining Software.' IEEE Transactions on Software Engineering, 2(1), 9–32. Hamilton coined 'software engineering' during her Apollo work (approximately 1968); the term gained widespread adoption after the NATO Software Engineering Conference (1968). Presidential Medal of Freedom 2016. The famous photograph of Hamilton standing next to the Apollo Guidance Computer source code printout (which was taller than she was) became iconic in computing history.

entries.label: Tim Berners-Lee
entries.hint: Berners-Lee invented the World Wide Web at CERN as a tool for sharing particle physics documentation — and then made it royalty-free. His three key inventions — HTTP (the transfer protocol), HTML (the markup language), and URLs (the addressing scheme) — created the web by combining hypertext with the internet. His decision not to patent the web technologies and CERN's release of them into the public domain in 1993 were decisive in enabling its adoption across the world.
entries.note: Berners-Lee, T. (1989). 'Information Management: A Proposal.' CERN internal memo — the original web proposal. Berners-Lee, T. (1992). 'The World-Wide Web.' Computer Networks and ISDN Systems, 25. Turing Award 2016. Berners-Lee founded the World Wide Web Consortium (W3C, 1994) to standardise web technologies. He has become a prominent critic of the web's current trajectory — citing surveillance capitalism, misinformation, and data monopolies — and advocates for a 'Contract for the Web' and his Solid platform for decentralised data ownership.

entries.label: Shafi Goldwasser
entries.hint: Goldwasser, with Silvio Micali and Charles Rackoff, invented zero-knowledge proofs in 1985 — one of the most consequential results in modern cryptography. She also introduced probabilistic encryption, giving the first formal definition of semantic security: a ciphertext reveals nothing about the plaintext beyond its length. Her work established the mathematical framework that underpins modern cryptographic protocols, from secure authentication to blockchain privacy.
entries.note: Goldwasser, S., Micali, S. & Rackoff, C. (1985). 'The Knowledge Complexity of Interactive Proof Systems.' STOC '85, 291–304. Goldwasser, S. & Micali, S. (1984). 'Probabilistic Encryption.' Journal of Computer and System Sciences, 28(2), 270–299. Turing Award 2012 (Goldwasser and Micali jointly). Goldwasser has held joint appointments at MIT and the Weizmann Institute; she mentored many of the leading cryptographers of the subsequent generation. Her work on interactive proofs also contributed to the PCP theorem, which shows that mathematical proofs can be probabilistically verified by reading a constant number of bits.

entries.label: Geoffrey Hinton
entries.hint: Hinton championed neural networks through decades of scepticism, co-developing backpropagation (1986), Boltzmann machines (1985), and dropout regularisation (2012). The AlexNet paper (Krizhevsky, Sutskever, Hinton 2012) won the ImageNet challenge by a margin so large it triggered the deep learning revolution. More than any other researcher, Hinton is responsible for the return of neural networks as the dominant approach in AI — and has since become a prominent voice warning about existential risks from AI systems.
entries.note: Krizhevsky, A., Sutskever, I. & Hinton, G. E. (2012). 'ImageNet Classification with Deep Convolutional Neural Networks.' Advances in NeurIPS, 25, 1097–1105 — AlexNet. Rumelhart, D. E., Hinton, G. E. & Williams, R. J. (1986). 'Learning Representations by Back-propagating Errors.' Nature, 323, 533–536. Turing Award 2018 (Hinton, LeCun, Bengio — the 'deep learning trio'). Hinton resigned from Google in 2023 to speak freely about AI risk, stating he regrets some of his life's work. He has advocated for a global pause on frontier AI training and for international governance frameworks.

categories.label: Debates & Open Questions
categories.subtitle: Live controversies — where the field is actively contesting the evidence

entries.label: P vs NP: The Unsolved Central Problem
entries.hint: The P vs NP question asks whether every problem whose solution can be verified quickly can also be solved quickly. If P = NP, modern cryptography (which relies on problems being hard to solve) would collapse; integer factorisation, protein folding, and optimal scheduling could be solved efficiently. If P ≠ NP (the consensus view), no algorithm can bridge the gap between verifying and finding solutions to NP-complete problems. After 50 years and thousands of failed proof attempts, neither answer has been established.
entries.note: Cook, S. A. (1971) established NP-completeness. Gödel wrote to von Neumann in 1956 asking essentially the same question. Most complexity theorists believe P ≠ NP, but this belief is based on failed proof attempts rather than evidence of impossibility. Baker, Gill & Solovay (1975) proved that the question cannot be resolved by 'relativising' proof techniques (oracles) — ruling out the most common proof strategies. Aaronson, S. (2017). 'P vs. NP' in Open Problems in Mathematics, Springer — a comprehensive survey. The problem is worth USD 1 million (Clay Institute Millennium Prize).

entries.label: Can Machines Think? (The Chinese Room Debate)
entries.loc: University of California Berkeley, Berkeley, California
entries.hint: Searle's Chinese Room argument (1980) challenges the view that symbol manipulation constitutes genuine understanding: a person in a room follows rules to manipulate Chinese symbols without understanding Chinese — just as a computer manipulates symbols without understanding anything. Defenders of AI (Dennett, Hofstadter) argue Searle mislocates understanding: it is the system, not the person, that understands. The debate has intensified with large language models that produce fluent, apparently thoughtful text.
entries.note: Searle, J. R. (1980). 'Minds, Brains, and Programs.' Behavioral and Brain Sciences, 3, 417–424 (with peer commentary). Newell, A. & Simon, H. A. (1976) defended the symbolic view. Dennett, D. C. (1991). Consciousness Explained. Little, Brown — argues understanding is distributed across the system. The debate remains unresolved; LLMs have made it more acute because their outputs are indistinguishable from human text on many tasks while their internal computations are opaque. Chalmers, D. J. (2023). 'Could a Large Language Model be Conscious?' arXiv:2303.07103 — a recent engagement.

entries.label: AI Safety & Alignment
entries.loc: Future of Life Institute, Cambridge, Massachusetts
entries.hint: As AI systems grow more capable, ensuring they reliably pursue intended goals becomes increasingly difficult. The alignment problem asks: how do we specify what we want from an AI system, and ensure it does that and only that? Proposed failure modes include reward hacking (optimising a proxy measure rather than the intended goal), deceptive alignment (appearing aligned during training but not in deployment), and instrumental convergence (advanced systems acquiring resources and resisting shutdown as subgoals). The field ranges from near-term safety engineering to long-run existential risk.
entries.note: Bostrom, N. (2014). Superintelligence: Paths, Dangers, Strategies. Oxford University Press — framed existential risk. Russell, S. (2019). Human Compatible: Artificial Intelligence and the Problem of Control. Viking. Open Letter on AI Safety (2015, Future of Life Institute) — signed by Hawking, Musk, and leading researchers. Bengio, Y. et al. (2024). 'Managing Extreme AI Risks amid Rapid Progress.' Science, 384, 842–845.
