Sceptics: LeCun, Y. argues current LLMs are not on a path to dangerous superintelligence and that the risks are overstated. The debate divides between short-term harms (bias, misuse) and long-term existential scenarios.

entries.label: Crypto Wars: Encryption vs Surveillance
entries.loc: National Security Agency, Fort Meade, Maryland
entries.hint: The Crypto Wars debate asks how much the state can or should limit strong encryption. The US government proposed the Clipper Chip (1993) — a hardware key-escrow system that would give law enforcement a backdoor to encrypted communications — triggering fierce opposition from technologists and civil liberties groups. The debate recurs: each generation of end-to-end encrypted messaging (PGP, Signal, iMessage) prompts law enforcement demands for backdoors, which cryptographers argue would weaken security for everyone.
entries.note: US Government (1993). Clipper Chip proposal — EES standard with key escrow for government access. Blaze, M. (1994). 'Protocol Failure in the Escrowed Encryption Standard.' CCS '94 — showed the Clipper design was flawed. Zimmermann, P. created PGP (1991) as a direct counter to government encryption controls; he was investigated for 'munitions export violations' until the case was dropped. Post-Snowden (2013), public support for encryption strengthened; Apple's refusal to create an iPhone backdoor for the FBI (2016) is the contemporary case. Mathematically, backdoors weaken security against all adversaries, not just authorised ones.

entries.label: Open Source vs Proprietary Software
entries.hint: Richard Stallman's GNU Manifesto (1983) argued that software should be free — not in price but in freedom to use, study, modify, and redistribute. The debate between open and proprietary development models has played out across operating systems (Linux vs Windows), databases (PostgreSQL vs Oracle), and AI (open-weight vs closed models). Open source has won many battles (the web stack, mobile, cloud) but failed others (desktop office software); the rise of AI has reopened the debate with new stakes around safety.
entries.note: Stallman, R. M. (1983). 'The GNU Manifesto.' Dr. Dobb's Journal. Raymond, E. S. (1999). The Cathedral and the Bazaar. O'Reilly — contrasted top-down (cathedral) and community-driven (bazaar) development models. Torvalds, L. created Linux in 1991 as a free Unix-compatible kernel; combined with GNU tools it became the dominant server OS. The debate has re-opened around large AI models: Meta's Llama models (partially open-weight) are positioned against OpenAI's closed GPT series; the safety implications of open-weight frontier models are actively contested.

entries.label: Net Neutrality & Internet Governance
entries.loc: Columbia University Law School, New York, New York
entries.hint: Net neutrality — the principle that internet service providers must treat all data packets equally, regardless of source, destination, or content type — was articulated by Tim Wu in 2003. Without it, ISPs can throttle competitors, charge for fast lanes, or block services. With it, the internet preserves an open platform for innovation. The principle has been implemented, repealed, and reimplemented multiple times in the US; it remains law in the EU but is contested globally.
entries.note: Wu, T. (2003). 'Network Neutrality, Broadband Discrimination.' Journal of Telecommunications and High Technology Law, 2, 141–179 — coined the term. FCC Open Internet Order (2015) implemented net neutrality in the US; repealed by the FCC in 2017 (Pai chairmanship); partially reinstated in 2024. EU: Open Internet Regulation (2015/2120) is in force. Opponents argue that ISPs should be able to charge for quality-of-service guarantees to fund network investment. Proponents argue that discrimination would entrench incumbents and suppress startup competition.

entries.label: Software Crisis & Technical Debt
entries.hint: The 1968 NATO Software Engineering Conference declared a 'software crisis': programs were late, over budget, unreliable, and unmaintainable. The term 'technical debt' (Cunningham 1992) extended this: shortcuts taken to ship faster create future maintenance costs, like financial debt with interest. Decades later, the crisis persists in new forms — software systems are larger and more complex than ever, security vulnerabilities are pervasive, and the gap between what software promises and what it reliably delivers remains substantial.
entries.note: Naur, P. & Randell, B. (Eds.) (1969). 'Software Engineering: Report on a Conference Sponsored by the NATO Science Committee.' NATO Scientific Affairs Division. Cunningham, W. (1992). 'The WyCash Portfolio Management System.' OOPSLA Experience Report — introduced 'technical debt'. Brooks, F. P. (1987). 'No Silver Bullet.' IEEE Computer, 20(4), 10–19 — argued that no single invention would produce order-of-magnitude improvements in software productivity. The Chaos Report (Standish Group, annually since 1994) documents software project failure rates, typically 30–40% outright failure and ~50% significant overrun — figures contested methodologically but indicative of persistent problems.

entries.label: Reproducibility Crisis in Machine Learning
entries.loc: Montreal Institute for Learning Algorithms, Montreal, Quebec
entries.hint: ML researchers have documented systematic failures of reproducibility: papers report state-of-the-art results that cannot be matched by independent teams following the published procedure. Sources include unreported hyperparameter search, cherry-picked random seeds, inconsistent baseline comparisons, and missing code. The crisis is particularly acute in reinforcement learning and NLP benchmarks, where small implementation details can account for large performance swings — a problem that is obscured when code is not released.
entries.note: Lucic, M. et al. (2018). 'Are GANs Created Equal? A Large-Scale Study.' NeurIPS 31. Henderson, P. et al. (2018). 'Deep Reinforcement Learning That Matters.' AAAI 32 — showed RL results often fail to reproduce even from the same codebase with different random seeds. Pineau, J. et al. (2021). 'Improving Reproducibility in Machine Learning Research (A Report from the NeurIPS 2019 Reproducibility Program).' JMLR, 22, 1–20. Mitigations: NeurIPS and ICML now require code submission; the ML Reproducibility Challenge (2018–present) replicates accepted papers. Schaeffer, R. et al. (2023). 'Are Emergent Abilities of Large Language Models a Mirage?' NeurIPS — argues that apparent emergent capabilities are artefacts of nonlinear evaluation metrics.

entries.label: Reasoning Models and Agentic AI: The 2024–25 Inflection
entries.hint: The 2024 release of OpenAI o1 — trained via reinforcement learning to produce explicit chain-of-thought reasoning before answering — marked a claimed qualitative shift from fluent generation to deliberate, "System 2" reasoning. DeepSeek-R1 (January 2025) reproduced comparable performance at a fraction of the training cost, raising questions about model efficiency and geopolitical AI competition. Simultaneously, agentic frameworks enabled AI systems to take multi-step autonomous actions over extended time horizons. Whether these developments constitute a new computational paradigm or are incremental scaling is actively contested among researchers.
entries.note: OpenAI (2024). "Learning to Reason with LLMs." openai.com — describes o1. Guo, D. et al. (2025). "DeepSeek-R1: Incentivizing Reasoning Capability in LLMs via Reinforcement Learning." arXiv:2501.12948. Three key contested claims: (1) Whether chain-of-thought training instils genuine reasoning or sophisticated pattern-matching on reasoning-shaped text — Kambhampati et al. (2024) argue the latter; (2) Whether current architectures can sustain reliable multi-step autonomous action without compounding errors across long horizons; (3) Whether open-weight reasoning models (DeepSeek-R1, Llama reasoning variants) create unacceptable proliferation risks versus democratising capability. Benchmark saturation: models exceeding 90% on AIME, MATH, and GPQA signals that existing evaluations are insufficient for frontier tracking (Chollet 2024, ARC-AGI-2 challenge). Regulatory responses — EU AI Act risk tiers, US executive orders on frontier AI — are still being calibrated to this generation of systems.

categories.label: Applications
categories.subtitle: Where the science leaves the lab

entries.label: GPS: Satellite Navigation
entries.loc: US Naval Research Laboratory, Washington, D.C.
entries.hint: The Global Positioning System uses a constellation of 24+ satellites broadcasting precise time signals; a receiver calculates its position by measuring signal arrival-time differences from at least four satellites. GPS was made fully operational in 1995 and opened to civilian use (SA turned off) in 2000. It is now embedded in smartphones, aviation, precision agriculture, financial transaction timestamps, and autonomous vehicles — a case where fundamental physics (general relativity corrections are required for GPS accuracy) meets everyday engineering.
entries.note: Bradford Parke & Roger Easton (NRL, 1960s) and Ivan Getting (Aerospace Corp) are the primary inventors. Easton filed the foundational patents; Parke led the TIMATION program. Full Operational Capability declared 1995; Selective Availability (intentional accuracy degradation for civilians) disabled 2000. Relativity note: GPS satellite clocks must be adjusted for both special relativity (time dilation from velocity, ~7 μs/day fast) and general relativity (gravitational time dilation, ~45 μs/day slow); net effect ~38 μs/day faster — without corrections, GPS would accumulate ~10 km/day of position error.

entries.label: SSL/TLS & Secure E-Commerce
entries.hint: Netscape's SSL (Secure Sockets Layer, 1994) protocol provided the first widely deployed mechanism for encrypting web transactions — enabling e-commerce by assuring users that credit card numbers sent over the internet were protected. SSL combined public-key cryptography (RSA) for key exchange with symmetric encryption (RC4, later AES) for data transfer. Its successor TLS is now mandatory for all financial, health, and government web services, protecting an estimated 7 trillion USD in annual web transactions.
entries.note: Hickman, K. (1995). 'The SSL Protocol.' Netscape Communications — internal draft. SSL 2.0 (Netscape 1995) was the first deployed version; SSL 3.0 (1996) fixed its weaknesses. IETF standardised TLS 1.0 in 1999 (RFC 2246), built on SSL 3.0. SSL 2.0 and 3.0 and TLS 1.0/1.1 are now deprecated due to known attacks (BEAST, POODLE). TLS 1.3 (RFC 8446, 2018) eliminated weak cipher suites and introduced 0-RTT resumption. Let's Encrypt (2015) made free TLS certificates available to anyone, driving HTTPS adoption from ~33% of web pages in 2015 to ~90% by 2024.

entries.label: Recommendation Systems
entries.hint: Collaborative filtering — the basis of modern recommendation engines — was first implemented in Tapestry (Xerox PARC, 1994): users' past ratings predict future preferences, and users with similar histories are used to make predictions for one another. Amazon (1999), Netflix (2006), Spotify, and YouTube built systems that account for a significant fraction of their consumption. The Netflix Prize (2006–2009, USD 1M) accelerated the field, producing matrix factorisation methods that raised accuracy by 10%.
entries.note: Goldberg, D. et al. (1992). 'Using Collaborative Filtering to Weave an Information Tapestry.' CACM, 35(12), 61–70 — coined 'collaborative filtering.' Koren, Y., Bell, R. & Volinsky, C. (2009). 'Matrix Factorization Techniques for Recommender Systems.' IEEE Computer, 42(8), 30–37 — the Netflix Prize winning approach. Filter bubbles: Pariser, E. (2011). The Filter Bubble — argued that personalised recommendations create ideological echo chambers. Empirical evidence for this effect is contested (Guess et al. 2019, Science). Modern recommenders use deep learning (two-tower models, transformers); the algorithms are largely proprietary and poorly documented.

entries.label: Search Engines & Web Indexing
entries.hint: Web search requires indexing billions of documents (inverted index), ranking them by relevance (PageRank and hundreds of other signals), and returning results in under 100 milliseconds. Google's early breakthrough was link-based ranking (PageRank) combined with anchor-text analysis; modern search layers machine learning (BERT, MUM) to understand query intent rather than just match keywords. Search engines have become the primary interface between humans and organised knowledge.
entries.note: Brin, S. & Page, L. (1998). 'The Anatomy of a Large-Scale Hypertextual Web Search Engine.' WWW7, 107–117. Google processes approximately 8.5 billion searches per day (2024). The inverted index — mapping each term to the list of documents containing it — is the core data structure; Google's Bigtable (Chang et al. 2006, OSDI) and MapReduce (Dean & Ghemawat 2004, OSDI) were built to scale indexing to web size. Modern ranking is a black box: the Helpful Content Update and various core updates show that search ranking is continuously contested between platforms and publishers.

entries.label: Large Language Models in Production (ChatGPT)
entries.hint: ChatGPT (OpenAI, November 2022) demonstrated that conversational AI based on large language models could pass professional exams, write production code, draft legal documents, and tutor students — reaching 100 million users in two months, the fastest adoption of any consumer application in history. LLMs are now embedded in search engines, word processors, coding environments, and customer service at scale. The applications reveal both extraordinary capability and systematic failure modes: hallucination, sycophancy, and unpredictable behaviour at distribution boundaries.
entries.note: OpenAI (2023). 'GPT-4 Technical Report.' arXiv:2303.08774 — describes GPT-4, the model underlying ChatGPT's March 2023 update. Anthropic's Claude, Google's Gemini, and Meta's Llama are competing systems. Key benchmarks: MMLU, HumanEval (coding), MATH. Documented failure modes: Maynez, J. et al. (2020). 'On Faithfulness and Factuality in Abstractive Summarization.' ACL — hallucination is intrinsic to next-token prediction, not an engineering accident. Bang, Y. et al. (2023). 'A Multitask, Multilingual, Multimodal Evaluation of ChatGPT.' arXiv:2302.04023 — documents cross-domain capability and limitations.

entries.label: Cloud Computing (Amazon Web Services)
entries.hint: Amazon Web Services (2006) commercialised the idea that computing infrastructure — servers, storage, databases, networking — could be rented by the hour over the internet rather than purchased and managed on-premises. EC2 (elastic compute) and S3 (object storage) were the founding services. Cloud computing enabled startups to build globally scalable systems without capital infrastructure and fundamentally restructured the economics of software deployment, concentrating computing power in a small number of hyperscaler data centres.
entries.note: Armbrust, M. et al. (2010). 'A View of Cloud Computing.' CACM, 53(4), 50–58 — the canonical academic framing. AWS launched EC2 (August 2006) and S3 (March 2006). AWS held ~31% of global cloud market share in 2024 (Synergy Research); Azure (~24%) and Google Cloud (~12%) are the other major players. Cloud concentration creates systemic risk: the 2021 AWS us-east-1 outage took down Netflix, Slack, and thousands of services simultaneously. Latency, data sovereignty, and carbon footprint are active concerns; data centre electricity consumption is estimated at ~1–2% of global electricity use (IEA 2023).

entries.label: Ethereum and Programmable Blockchain
entries.hint: Vitalik Buterin extended the Bitcoin blockchain with a Turing-complete scripting language, enabling arbitrary programs — smart contracts — to execute on-chain and self-enforce their terms with no trusted intermediary. A loan, insurance policy, or voting procedure encoded as a smart contract runs deterministically across thousands of nodes simultaneously. Ethereum became the platform for decentralised finance (DeFi), non-fungible tokens (NFTs), and layer-2 scaling — demonstrating that blockchain is programmable infrastructure, not merely digital currency.
entries.note: Buterin, V. (2014). "Ethereum: A Next-Generation Smart Contract and Decentralised Application Platform." ethereum.org white paper. Wood, G. (2014). "Ethereum: A Secure Decentralised Generalised Transaction Ledger." Yellow Paper — the technical specification. Ethereum's transition from proof-of-work to proof-of-stake ("The Merge," September 2022) reduced energy consumption by ~99.95%. Critiques: smart contracts are immutable once deployed, making bugs catastrophic — the DAO hack (2016, ~USD 60M drained via a reentrancy vulnerability) forced a controversial hard fork. Solidity, the dominant smart-contract language, has accumulated a taxonomy of well-known security pitfalls (reentrancy, integer overflow, front-running). Regulatory status of DeFi tokens and smart-contract enforceability remain legally contested globally. As of 2025, Ethereum processes over 1 million transactions per day across layer-1 and major layer-2 networks (Arbitrum, Optimism, Base).
